title: News outlets ask court to sanction OpenAI, citing hidden discovery evidence
subtitle: The New York Times, The Intercept, Ziff Davis, and three other publishers moved for sanctions in the ChatGPT copyright suit, alleging OpenAI concealed for two years that it could search its own training data and output logs.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On July 9, 2026, the New York Times, the Daily News, the Center for Investigative Reporting, The Intercept, and Ziff Davis filed a motion for sanctions against OpenAI in the copyright litigation pending before the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York. The plaintiffs allege that OpenAI concealed for more than two years its ability to search the training datasets and output logs of its models — capabilities the plaintiffs had specifically requested in discovery — and separately deleted billions of ChatGPT conversations that the court had ordered preserved. The motion asks for penalties ranging from monetary fines to adverse-inference jury instructions.

The trigger was a February 2026 deposition of Vinnie Monaco, who leads privacy engineering at OpenAI. Monaco testified that OpenAI had in fact searched training data and output records during the litigation — directly contradicting the company's prior representations to the court that it could not. Adverse-inference sanctions, if granted, would let the jury assume the concealed evidence supported the plaintiffs' theory. In a case that turns on what OpenAI ingested to train GPT and whether the resulting outputs reproduce protected expression, that presumption could be decisive.

The Times sued OpenAI and Microsoft in December 2023, the first major news outlet to bring a copyright action against a generative-AI company. Since then more than a dozen publishers, authors, and record labels have filed similar suits. Two 2025 rulings — Judge William Alsup in Bartz v. Anthropic and Judge Vince Chhabria in Kadrey v. Meta — held that training on copyrighted text can qualify as fair use, but both sidestepped how misappropriated copies were obtained and preserved. The sanctions motion is aimed squarely at that softer flank.

Takeaway for learners: the AI industry has argued for two years that its systems are inscrutable — models cannot be introspected, training corpora cannot be reconstructed. That framing was always partly a business posture, and the Monaco deposition shows how quickly it collapses under discovery. If you build products on top of these models, assume every prompt and every generated response is searchable, producible, and auditable in a future proceeding — because they are.

sources.title: New York Times and Other News Outlets Accuse OpenAI of Lying in Discovery — Variety
